CF1.Name: Hidden-Figures-Test
Example: The answer is A. The figure below shows how figure A is included in the pattern.
The answer is D. The figure below shows how figure D is included in the pattern.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to tell whether a sample figure can be found in a more complex pattern. You will first see one simple figure. Then you will see a figure of the complex pattern. NOTE: If the figure is in the complex pattern, it will always be right side up and exactly the same size with the simple figure. You are to decide whether the first image can be found in the second image.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "yes_or_no"}.

CF2.Name: Hidden-Patterns-Test
Example: In the second image, when the model appears, it is shown by heavy lines. Therefore, the answer is: O, X, O, O, X, O, O. Now try this example:
The first, third, fourth, eighth, and tenth patterns contain the model. The second, fifth, sixth, seventh, and ninth do not contain the model. Therefore, the answer is: X, O, X, X, O, O, O, X, O, X.

Group.Description: How quickly can you recognize a figure that is hidden among other lines? Each problem in this test contains an image of a model and another image of a pattern. You are to look for the model in the first image from the pattern in the second image. The model must always be in the given position, not on its side or upside down. You are to answer "X" if the model appears in the pattern or answer "O" if the model does not appear.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "X_or_O"}.

CF3.Name: Copying-Test
Example: The answer is (5, 3). Here is another sample item. If you think it will help you, try blacking the correct dots first, like this:
The answer is (5, 4). Now try the following items.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to keep in mind a pattern so that you can quickly find it in a square of dots. You need to solve the problem by copying the pattern on the dots. The copied pattern must start with the circled dot, and every corner must come at one of the dots. When you are finished, it must look exactly like the original pattern. The dots are numbered as: the upper left dot is (1, 1) and the lower right dot is (5, 5), with the first number being the row number and the second number being the column number. You are to decide which dot will the pattern end in when starting from the circled dot.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Answer the (row, column) of the ending dot. Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "(Row_Number, Column_NUmber)"}.

CS1.Name: Gestalt-Completion-Test
Example: Look at each incomplete picture and try to see what it is.
This picture is a hammer head.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to see a whole picture even though it is not completely drawn. You are to use your imagination to fill in the missing parts.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: You need to use a word or two to describe it. Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "a_word_or_two"}.

CS2.Name: Concealed-Words-Test
Example: Look at the words printed below.

The word north has been completely printed on the above; one the below, parts of the letters have been erased. Now look at the sample items below. Parts of each word have been erased. Try to read what each word is.
The words are: 1. parents; 2. easy; and 3. giant.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to read a word when parts of the word have been erased. All the words used in this test will be at least four letters long. No word will contain any capital letters. You are to read what each word is.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "a_word"}.

CS3.Name: Snowy-Pictures
Example: Look at the picture below. What object do you see?
By looking carefully at this sample you will see an anchor. Now try another sample.
The picture shows a small boat sitting in the water. Boat, rowboat, or other similar words would be correct answers.

Group.Description: It is helpful to be able to see objects quickly in spite of their being partially concealed by snow, rain, haze, darkness, or other visual obstructions. In this test you are to recognize hard-to-see objects. Similar words would be considered correct answers.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please use a word to describe it. Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "a_word"}.

I3.Name: Figure-Classification
Example: Now look at the sample problem below.

The figures in the first image are divided into Group 1 and Group 2. The squares in Group 1 are shaded and the squares in Group 2 are not shaded. Therefore, the shaded squares in the second image belong to Group 1 and the unshaded squares belong to Group 2. The answers are: 2, 2, 1, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1.
Now try this more difficult sample, which has three groups.

The figures in Group 1 consist of both straight and curved lines. The figures in Group 2 consist of curved lines only. The figures in Group 3 consist of straight lines only. As you can see, there are other details that have nothing to do with the rule. The answers are: 1, 1, 3, 1, 2, 1, 2, 2.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to discover rules that explain things. In each problem on this test there are either two or three groups, each consisting of three figures. You will first see an image of the two or three groups, followed by another image of the figure to be classified. You are to look for something that is the same about the three figures in any one group and for things that make the groups different from one another. Then you need to decide which group should the figure in the second image belongs to.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "1_or_2_or_3"}.

P3.Name: Identical-Pictures-Test
Example: The third test object is the same as the object at the left. Now try this problem.
The second test object is the same as the object at the left. Now try this problem.
The fifth test object is the same as the object at the left. Now try this problem.
The fourth test object is the same as the object at the left. Now try this problem.
The second test object is the same as the object at the left.

Group.Description: How fast can you match a given object? This is a test of your ability to decide whether two objects are the same quickly. You will see two images of the two objects.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "yes_or_no"}.

S1.Name: Card-Rotations-Test
Example: Look at the 5 triangle-shaped cards drawn below.
All of these drawings are of the same card, which has been slid around into different positions on the page. Now look at the 2 cards below:
These two cards are not alike. The first cannot be made to look like the second by sliding it around on the page. It would have to be flipped over or made differently.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to see differences in figures. Each problem in this test consists of two cards. You are to decide whether the two cards are the same or different.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Answer "S" if they are the same. Answer "D" if they are different. Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "S_or_D"}.

S2.Name: Cube-Comparisons-Test
Example: Compare the two cubes in the pair below.

The pair is different because they must be drawings of different cubes. If the left cube is turned so that the A is upright and facing you, the N would be to the left of the A and hidden, not to the right of the A as is shown on the right hand member of the pair. Thus, the drawings must be of different cubes. Compare the two cubes in the pair below.

The pair is the same because they could be drawings of the same cube. That is, if the A is turned on its side the X becomes hidden, the B is now on top, and the C (which was hidden) now appears. Thus the two drawings could be of the same cube. Compare the two cubes in the pair below.
The pair is different because the X cannot be at the peak of the A on the left hand drawing and at the base of the A on the right hand drawing. Compare the two cubes in the pair below.
The pair is different because P has its side next to G on the left hand cube but its top next to G on the right hand cube. Compare the two cubes in the pair below.
The pair is the same because the J and K are just turned on their side, moving the O to the top.

Group.Description: Wooden blocks such as children play with are often cubical with a different letter, number, or symbol on each of the six faces (top, bottom, four sides). Each problem in this test consists of drawings of pairs of cubes or blocks of this kind. Remember, there is a different design, number, or letter on each face of a given cube or block. Note: No letters, numbers, or symbols appear on more than one face of a given cube. Except for that, any letter, number or symbol can be on the hidden faces of a cube. You are to decide whether the two cubes are the same or different after applying some rotations.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Answer "S" if the two cubes are the same. Answer "D" if the two cubes are different. Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "S_or_D"}.

SS2.Name: Choosing-a-Path
Example: In the picture below is an example box with dots marked S and F.

The first box is the one which has the line from S, through the circle, and back to F. The space at the lower right of box A has therefore been blackened to show that this box has the line through the circle.
The box D is the one which has the line from S, through the circle, and back to F.
The box B is the one which has the line from S, through the circle, and back to F.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to choose a correct path from among several choices. In the problems in this test there will be five boxes with two dots marked S and F. S is the starting point and F is the finish. You are to follow the line from S, through the circle at the top of the picture and back to F. Only one box will have a line from the S, through the circle, and back to the F in the same box. Dots on the lines show the only places where connections can be made between lines. If lines meet or cross where there is no dot, there is no connection between the lines. Each diagram in the test has only one box which has a line through the circle and back to the F. Some lines are wrong because they lead to a dead end. Some lines are wrong because they come back to the box without going through the circle. Some lines are wrong because they lead to other boxes that do not have lines going through the circle. You are to decide which box which has the line from S, through the circle, and back to F.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "A_or_B_or_C_or_D_or_E"}.

SS3.Name: Map-Planning-Test
Example: Look at the sample map below. Practice by finding the shortest route between the various points listed at the right of the map.

The answers to the other practice problems are as follows: 1. The shortest route from A to Z passes 1 buildings; 2. The shortest route from E to S passes 5 buildings; 3. The shortest route from P to J passes 3 buildings; 4. The shortest route from V to K passes 2 buildings; 5. The shortest route from O to F passes 4 buildings; 6. The shortest route from G to M passes 4 buildings; 7. The shortest route from D to Q passes 6 buildings; 8. The shortest route from F to T passes 5 buildings.

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to find the shortest route between two places as quickly as possible. The figure in each test is a map of a city. The dark lines are streets. The circles are road-blocks, and you cannot pass at the places where there are circles. The numbered squares are buildings. Rules: 1. The shortest route will always pass along the side of one and only one of the numbered buildings. 2. A building is not considered as having been passed if a route passes only a corner and not a side. 3. The same numbered building may be used on more than one route. You are to find the shortest route between two lettered points. The number on the building passed is your answer.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Answer the number of buildings that the shortest route passes. Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "a_number"}.
Additional: How many buildings does the shortest route from F to I pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from U to D pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from B to W pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Z to E pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from C to K pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from X to L pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Y to R pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from M to J pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from P to M pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from N to T pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from U to B pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from N to Q pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from S to X pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Y to P pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Y to G pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from F to L pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from A to E pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from K to D pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Q to C pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from N to H pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Y to R pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from E to X pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from L to F pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from D to K pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from W to Q pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from C to W pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from T to B pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from V to P pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from H to F pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from O to Z pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from S to Y pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from X to R pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from L to T pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from I to R pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from V to O pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from J to E pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from K to Q pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from F to C pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from Q to W pass?
How many buildings does the shortest route from D to K pass?

VZ1.Name: Form-Board-Test
Example: The rectangle can be made from the first, third, fourth, and fifth pieces. The second piece is not needed to make the rectangle. Therefore, the answer is [+, -, +, +, +]. The rectangle drawn below shows one way in which the four pieces could be put together.
The first, fourth, and fifth pieces are needed. The answer is [+, -, -, +, +].
The second, third, and fifth pieces are needed. The answer is [-, +, +, -, +].

Group.Description: This is a test of your ability to tell what pieces can be put together to make a certain figure. Each problem consists of one geometrical figure and another figure of a row of five shaded pieces. Any number of shaded pieces, from two to five, may be used to make the complete figure. Each piece may be turned around to any position but it cannot be turned over. It may help you to sketch the way the pieces fit together. You may use any blank space for doing this. Your task is to decide which of the five shaded pieces will make the complete figure when put together.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Answer "-" if the piece is not used and answer "+" if the piece is used. Your answer will be a list of five "-" or "+". Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": ["list_of_five_-_or_+"]}.

VZ2.Name: Paper-Folding-Test
Example: Now try the sample problem below. (In this problem only one hole was punched in the folded paper.)
The correct answer to the sample problems above is C. The figures below show how the paper was folded and why C is the correct answer.

Group.Description: In this test you are to imagine the folding and unfolding of pieces of paper. In each problem in the test you will see some figures first, representing a square piece of paper being folded, and the last of these figures has one or two small circles drawn on it to show where the paper has been punched. Each hole is punched through all the thicknesses of paper at that point. Then you will see one figure with holes. In these problems all of the folds that are made are shown in the figures you see first, and the paper is not turned or moved in any way except to make the folds shown in the figures. Remember, the second figure is correct only when it shows the positions of the holes when the paper is completely unfolded. You are to decide whether the second figure correctly shows where the holes will be when the paper is completely unfolded.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "yes_or_no"}.

VZ3.Name: Surface-Development-Test
Example: Now try the practice problem below.

In the above problem, if the side with edge 1 is folded around to form the back of the object, then edge 1 will be the same as edge H. If the side with edge 5 is folded back, then the side with edge 4 may be folded down so that edge 4 is the same as edge C. All answers are as follows: 1 is H; 2 is B; 3 is G; 4 is C; and 5 is H. Notice that two of the answers can be the same.

Group.Description: In this test you are to try to imagine or visualize how a piece of paper can be folded to form some kind of object. Each problem consists of two drawings. The drawing on the left is of a piece of paper which can be folded on the dotted lines to form the object drawn at the right. Note: The side of the flat piece marked with the X will always be the same as the side of the object marked with the X. Therefore, the paper must always be folded so that the X will be on the outside of the object. You are to imagine the folding and are to figure out which of the lettered edges on the object (second figure) are the same as the numbered edges on the piece of paper in the first figure.
Group.Instruction: For the question below: Please provide your answer in the following JSON format: {"answer": "a_letter"}.

MA1.Name: Picture-Number-Test
Example: Here are some practice pictures with numbers.
Then you will see pictures like these. For the first picture, the correct number is 10.

Split.Description: This is a test of your ability to learn picture-number combinations. You will first study a page of some pictures with numbers. After studying, you will be given a random picture. You will be asked to write down the numbers that go with them.
Split.Instruction: For the question below: Please write down the number that you can remember in the following JSON format: {"answers": "a_number"}.

MV1.Name: Shape-Memory-Test
Example: Now look at this sample. Study this picture first.
For each sample picture below, answer Y (Yes) if the picture shows part of the picture you studied or N (No) if the picture is not one you studied.
You should have answered N for picture I, Y for picture II, and Y for picture III.

Split.Description: This is a test of your ability to remember a group of shapes and their positions in relation to each other. You will be given a picture of different shapes to study. After you learn the shapes and their orientation, you will be given another picture and asked whether it is the same as part of the shapes in the first picture or whether it is different from those.
Split.Instruction: Remember the test pictures will show only a small part of the picture that was studied. Answer Y for pictures that show the same shapes in the same orientation as on the study picture. Answer N for pictures that show different shapes. For the question below: Please answer in the following JSON format: {"answers": "Y_or_N"}.

MV2.Name: Building-Memory
Example: Now look at this simple and enlarged sample. Study this map first.
Now look at this empty map:
Now you are given some houses. For each picture answer the letter that corresponds with where each house was located on the study map.
Your answers for sample picture 1 should be A, for 2, C, and for 3, B.

Split.Description: This is a test of your ability to remember the position of things on a street map. You will be given a map with streets and buildings and other structures to study. After you learn the street layout and the different kinds of structures, you will be given an empty street map and a picture of a structure. You will be asked where the structure was located on the study map.
Split.Instruction: For the question below: Please answer in the following JSON format: {"answers": "A_or_B_or_C_or_D_or_E"}.
